title: Add prediction mode — 'what happens next' with multiple-choice before each step
description: The single most impactful practice feature. Based on Roediger & Karpicke 2006: active recall retains 80% vs 34% for passive viewing. Also validated by dpvis (SIGCSE 2025, 75% student improvement). Add toggle 'Predict Mode' in playback controls. When enabled, pause before each step, show 3 options (correct + 2 plausible distractors). Track correct/total. For sorting: 'Which two elements are compared next?' For graph: 'Which node is visited next?' For DP: 'What value goes in this cell?' Start with Bubble Sort, BFS, Knapsack as proof-of-concept. Prediction questions defined per-algorithm in a new predictions field on engine output.
acceptanceCriteria: Predict Mode toggle visible in playback section when result exists
In Predict Mode, animation pauses before EACH step
3 multiple-choice options shown — one correct, two plausible distractors
Correct answer: green flash + brief WHY explanation + score increment
Wrong answer: red flash + explanation of correct answer using visualization state
Score tracked as X/Y correct, displayed in playback area
Works for at least Bubble Sort, BFS, and Knapsack initially

title: Add manual tracing mode — user clicks elements instead of algorithm
description: Transform passive watching into active doing. User toggles 'Trace Mode'. Instead of the algorithm executing, the USER clicks which elements to compare/swap/visit. Tool validates each click: correct = advance, wrong = show why and let retry. For sorting: click two bars to compare, then click to swap if needed. For graph: click the node to visit next. Validates against the algorithm's actual next step. Start with Bubble Sort and BFS. Based on Bloom's 'Apply' level — can the student DO the algorithm, not just watch it?
acceptanceCriteria: Trace Mode toggle visible for Bubble Sort and BFS
In Trace Mode, bars/nodes become clickable
Clicking the correct next pair to compare: green highlight + advance
Clicking wrong pair: red highlight + hint about what the algorithm would do
Running count of correct/wrong traces
At completion: summary comparing user's trace vs algorithm's trace

title: Add comparison challenge prompts — guided side-by-side analysis
description: Existing comparison mode (AlgorithmPanel.tsx:300-305) lets users run two sorts but provides NO guidance. Add guided comparison challenges: (1) Before running, ask 'Which will finish with fewer comparisons?' (2) After running, show results side-by-side with explanation. (3) Pre-built challenges: 'Bubble vs Selection on nearly-sorted', 'Quick vs Merge on random', 'Quick vs Merge on sorted'. Targets Bloom's 'Analyze' level.
acceptanceCriteria: Pre-built comparison challenges accessible from a 'Challenge' button
Each challenge asks a prediction question before running
After both algorithms complete, results shown side-by-side
Explanation text shows WHY one was faster: 'Bubble Sort benefits from early exit on nearly-sorted data'
At least 5 pre-built comparison challenges covering sorting, graph, DP

title: Add debugging challenges — find the bug in broken algorithm implementations
description: Show a BROKEN implementation (pseudocode with a deliberate bug) alongside its visualization showing wrong behavior. Student identifies the bug line. Targets Bloom's 'Evaluate' level. Design 3 initial challenges: (1) Bubble Sort without early-exit on sorted input (O(n²) instead of O(n)), (2) Dijkstra accepting negative edges (wrong distances), (3) Quick Sort always picking first element (O(n²) on sorted). Each shows the buggy behavior, then asks 'What's wrong and how would you fix it?'
acceptanceCriteria: Debug Challenge accessible from a 'Challenges' section in sidebar
Buggy pseudocode displayed with line numbers
Visualization runs the buggy version showing incorrect behavior
Student clicks the buggy line to identify the error
Correct identification: explanation of the fix + comparison with correct version
At least 3 debugging challenges available

title: Add scenario-based algorithm selection questions
description: Present real-world scenarios and ask which algorithm/data structure to use. Targets Bloom's 'Evaluate' level and interview prep. Design 10 scenarios: GPS routing (Dijkstra), social network degrees (BFS), autocomplete (Trie), nearly-sorted logs (Insertion Sort), membership check (Bloom Filter), etc. Each has: scenario description, 4 algorithm options, correct answer with WHY explanation.
acceptanceCriteria: Scenario challenges accessible from sidebar 'Practice' section
Each scenario: real-world description + 4 algorithm choices
Correct answer: explanation of WHY this algorithm fits
Wrong answer: explanation of WHY this algorithm doesn't work for this scenario
At least 10 scenarios covering all 7 algorithm categories
Scenarios link to the relevant algorithm for immediate exploration

title: Add XP scoring system with streaks and mastery levels per algorithm
description: Duolingo research: streaks increase commitment by 60%, XP leaderboards drive 40% more engagement, 7-day streak users are 3.6x more likely to stay. Add: XP earned per action (5 for watching, 10-15 for correct prediction, 25 for trace, 30 for debug challenge). Per-algorithm mastery 0-5 stars. Daily streak counter. Store in localStorage with optional sync. Display as compact XP bar in sidebar header.
acceptanceCriteria: XP counter visible in sidebar header, increments on each action
Per-algorithm star rating (0-5) visible in algorithm dropdown
Daily streak counter with celebration animation on continuation
Streak freeze: missing one day doesn't break streak if used
Mastery levels: 0=untouched, 1=watched, 2=3/5 predictions, 3=5/5, 4=traced, 5=challenge solved
All progress persisted in localStorage

title: Add spaced repetition for algorithm review — resurface concepts after 1/3/7/14 days
description: Dunlosky et al. (2013) rated spaced repetition as 'high utility' — the only technique besides active recall. After completing an algorithm, schedule a review quiz (3 quick recall questions) after 1 day, 3 days, 7 days, 14 days. Difficulty adapts: cards answered correctly appear less often, wrong answers appear sooner. Use localStorage for scheduling. Show 'Review Due' indicator in sidebar when reviews are available.
acceptanceCriteria: After completing an algorithm, review questions scheduled at 1/3/7/14 day intervals
Review Due indicator visible in sidebar when reviews are available
Each review: 3 quick questions (complexity recall, step prediction, 'when to use')
Correct answers extend interval, wrong answers shorten it
Review history tracked in localStorage
Dismissable — user can skip reviews without penalty

title: Add complexity recall flashcards — quick-fire 'what is O(?) for X?' quiz
description: Bloom's Level 1 (Remember) has 1/5 support. Add quick-fire flashcard mode: show algorithm name, student types/selects time and space complexity. Immediate feedback. Track accuracy per algorithm. Can be used as warm-up before deeper practice. 62 flashcards (one per algorithm). Also include: 'Is X stable?', 'Is X in-place?', 'What data structure does X use?'
acceptanceCriteria: Flashcard mode accessible from sidebar 'Practice' section
Each card: algorithm name + question (time complexity / space / stable / in-place)
Student selects from options or types answer
Immediate green/red feedback with correct answer
Accuracy tracked per algorithm and per question type
Quick-fire mode: 10 random cards in 60 seconds

title: Add hints system for wrong answers — 3-level progressive hints
description: Practice without good feedback is useless. When a student answers wrong in any practice mode: Level 1 hint = rephrase the question ('Think about what BFS does with its queue...'). Level 2 hint = give a clue about the algorithm's strategy ('BFS always processes the OLDEST item in the queue first...'). Level 3 hint = show the first part and ask about the rest ('The queue is [C, D, E]. C was added first. What does FIFO mean?'). Design 3 levels of hints for each prediction/challenge type.
acceptanceCriteria: Wrong answers show 'Hint' button instead of just 'Wrong'
3 progressive hint levels, each revealing more information
Hints are specific to the algorithm and current state — not generic
Using hints reduces XP earned (full XP for first-attempt correct, 50% after 1 hint, 25% after 2)
Hint content for all prediction types defined in practice config

title: Add practice dashboard showing per-concept mastery and Bloom's level reached
description: Students need to SEE their progress. Add a dashboard view showing: (1) per-algorithm mastery level (0-5 stars), (2) Bloom's level reached per algorithm (Remember→Understand→Apply→Analyze→Evaluate→Create), (3) Overall module completion %, (4) Weakness identification: 'You struggle with DP — practice Knapsack again', (5) Streak counter and XP total. Based on Duolingo's progress dashboard design.
acceptanceCriteria: Dashboard accessible from sidebar header or tab
Shows all 62 algorithms with mastery stars and Bloom's level
Filter by category, difficulty, or completion status
Weakness section highlights algorithms with <50% prediction accuracy
Next recommended algorithm based on learning path + weakness
XP total, streak, and session summary visible
